questions.question: You are departing an airport at 2347' MSL, the altimeter setting is 29.67" Hg, and the OAT is 75°F. The performance charts indicate that your aircraft will climb at 68 MPH indicated airspeed and 689 FPM. If you are departing runway 24 with a wind of 200° at 17 knots, what is the maximum climb gradient you can maintain?

You are flying an approach to runway 22 at 73 KIAS, descending through 900' MSL, and the temperature is 84°F. Winds are 180° at 14 kts, and the altimeter setting is 29.97"Hg. If you are descending at 650 FPM to stay on the glideslope, what is the descent angle?
Convert the climb gradient of 255 feet/nm into meters/km.

ATC issued an altitude crossing restriction but the pilot got distracted and forgot to start their descent. With 27 NM remaining, they are still at FL290 and need to descend to 17,000' MSL. Assume standard pressure and an average ground speed of 450 knots in the descent. What is the required descent angle?
Reference the previous question. What is the minimum descent rate needed to meet that restriction?
questions.context: Referenced question 3: ATC issued an altitude crossing restriction but the pilot got distracted and forgot to start their descent. With 27 NM remaining, they are still at FL290 and need to descend to 17,000' MSL. Assume standard pressure and an average ground speed of 450 knots in the descent. What is the required descent angle?

questions.question: You are arriving into Jackson Hole, WY (elev. 6,451') and in the event of a go-around you must be able to maintain a minimum climb of 255 ft/nm. If your approach speed is 136 knots and you maintain this speed on a go-around, what minimum rate of climb is required to meet this climb gradient? Assume calm wind.

There is a 485' obstruction 2,451' from the departure end of Runway 14 (5,400' long). Your uncorrected ground roll is 823' and the performance chart states "Reduce ground roll by 4% for every 10 knots of headwind." Winds are 110° at 9 knots. What rate of climb is required to clear the obstacle by 50'?
Referencing the previous question, what is your climb gradient if you are traveling at 142 KM/hr?
questions.context: Referenced question 19: Your Piper Cub is not equipped with a VSI, you want to know what your climb rate is. You time yourself and see that every 18 seconds you climb 100'. What is your climb rate in meters per second?

questions.question: The departure procedure requires a 340'/nm climb to 900'. If you fly at Vx (73 kts) to 900', what rate of climb is required? You are departing runway 10 and the wind is 140° at 13 kts. Assume IAS = TAS.

You are taking off from a 4,318' runway at 137' MSL, and your calculated ground roll is 732'. There is an obstruction up to 712' MSL that is 1.2 km from the runway threshold. What climb gradient is required to clear the obstruction by 200'?

The missed approach climb gradient requires a minimum gradient of 350 ft./nautical mile. If your indicated airspeed on the missed approach will be 170 knots, which results in a climb rate of 1,050 ft/min, will you meet the climb gradient requirements at 7,500 feet pressure altitude on a standard day?

choices.text: Yes, with 20 feet/nautical mile to spare
No, you will be 18 feet/nautical mile short
Yes, with just 4 feet/nautical mile to spare
No, you will be just 3 feet/nautical mile short

questions.correctChoice: No, you will be 18 feet/nautical mile short

questions.question: Taking off from a small 2,652' grass strip, you rotate with 782' remaining. If you cross the end of the runway at 50' what is your climb gradient?

A Cessna 152 pilot at the NIFA landings competition wants to takeoff and reach 400' AGL at the exact moment he crosses the end of the runway. They start the takeoff 1/4 way down a 6500' runway with an average ground speed of 67 knots and a 500' takeoff ground roll. With the VSI reading 555 FPM, does the pilot climb high enough?

choices.text: Yes, will cross exactly at 400' AGL at the end of the runway
No, the 152 will be 360' AGL at the end of the runway
No, the 152 will be 270' AGL at the end of the runway
Yes, the 152 will be 430' AGL at the end of the runway

questions.correctChoice: No, the 152 will be 360' AGL at the end of the runway

questions.question: Climbing out of Eagle, CO you are maintaining 82 KIAS and need to achieve 580'/NM. In calm winds, what climb rate do you need to maintain? (Outside air temperature is 94°F and altimeter setting is 30.24" Hg).

You need to clear a ridge 8 nautical miles away that is 2,322 feet MSL at the summit. If you would like to clear the ridge by 500 feet, what minimum climb rate will you need if you expect an average ground speed of 84 knots departing from an airport elevation of 1,103' MSL?

Despite how you answered above, you elect to depart on runway 33. Can you clear an obstacle 10.6 NM off the departure end of the runway that is 9,151' tall? The aircraft climbs at 65 KTAS and 290 FPM.

choices.text: Yes, barely clear it by 6 feet
No, you need to climb 1361 feet more
No, you need to climb 1138 feet more

questions.correctChoice: Yes, barely clear it by 6 feet
questions.context: Referenced question 12: You are in Sun Valley, ID (5320') and the METAR reports winds are 270° at 32 knots. Your aircraft limitation is a 10-knot tailwind. Can you accept a departure off runway 15? (variation 13° E).

questions.question: There is a hill 1.5 nautical miles off the departure end of the runway that is 800' above airport elevation. On top of this hill they have built a cell tower that is 200' tall. If you want to clear this tower by 100' what climb gradient will you need to maintain?
Based on the previous question, if you plan to climb at 90 MPH what vertical speed will this require assuming you are flying into a constant 10 MPH headwind?
questions.context: Referenced question 26: There is a hill 1.5 nautical miles off the departure end of the runway that is 800' above airport elevation. On top of this hill they have built a cell tower that is 200' tall. If you want to clear this tower by 100' what climb gradient will you need to maintain?

questions.question: You are trying to figure out if you will be able to clear the construction crane just off the departure end of runway 36 at your home airport which has an elevation of 1,233 feet MSL. The ATIS is reporting the winds to be 320° at 13 knots, altimeter of 30.65" Hg, and the temperature is 18°C. The NOTAM says that the crane is 844 feet tall and is located just 1.5 NM from the end of the runway. If your best angle of climb speed is 75 KIAS, what rate of climb will you need to clear the crane?

While flying in the Swiss Alps, your departure procedure calls for a minimum climb gradient of 310'/km from the surface to 3,400 meters MSL. Assuming calm wind, what is the minimum climb rate you must maintain if you climb at 105 KTAS?
What climb rate is required if you are to maintain a climb gradient of 340 feet per nautical mile, while climbing at a true airspeed of 82 knots? Winds are calm.

You read in the POH for your Cessna 172 that in a glide, your descent gradient will be 600'/sm. If you are flying at 9,000' MSL and the terrain in the area is at 7,258 feet above sea level, what is your glide range?
There is a hill 1.8 NM from the runway that is 645' above airport elevation. Climbing at 75 KTS ground speed what minimum climb rate do you need to clear the top by 500'?

Departing Reno, NV (elev. 4,415') requires a climb gradient of 740' per nautical mile. On a departure course of 164° you have an indicated airspeed of 180 MPH. With calm winds and a standard atmosphere, what is the climb rate required to meet the requirement?

San Francisco's (elevation 13 feet) EUGEN9 departure requires that aircraft cross 4.0 DME from the SFO VOR at 1,900 ft. Assuming you lift off directly abeam the VOR, if your aircraft climbs at a ground speed of 158 mph, what climb rate must you have in order to meet this requirement?

Along your trip you got sidetracked trying to make Lester behave and you realized you had been cutting into your fuel reserves. Then, 5 miles from the runway you lose both of your engines. You pull back to 90 knots (your best glide speed) and descend down toward the runway at 550 FT/MIN. What is your rate of descent in FT/NM?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 25-28. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 25: It is a beautiful afternoon and you decide you are going to fly from Cherokee, Iowa to Des Moines, Iowa (TC = 132°) to pick up your three cousins and fly them around to see some farmland. Billy Jo weighs 45.3 kilograms, Sally Sue weighs 54.4 kilograms, and Cletus weighs 81.8 kilograms. For weight and balance purposes, how many pounds combined do your three cousins weigh?

questions.question: At what climb rate would an aircraft be assured to clear a 257' obstacle by 100' if the aircraft climbs from ground level at 80 knots ground speed and the obstacle is 0.74 SM away?

The departure procedure off of Rwy 30L in Dubai states you must maintain a climb gradient of 305 feet/nm to 2,000' MSL. Your climbout speed today is 162 KIAS. The winds are calm at this sea level airport with an OAT of 115°F. What vertical speed must you maintain to 2,000' MSL to comply with this departure procedure?

The O'Hare Six departure requires you to cross the 5 DME arc from the VOR at 3,000' MSL. Your planned runway for departure will have you rotate on the 1 DME arc from the VOR as you climb away from the station. Airport elevation is 650'. Assuming a straight out departure, what climb rate must you maintain if your average ground speed is 200 knots?

You must clear a 1,700' hilltop on departure. If you takeoff from sea level and the hill is 6.3 nm away, will you clear the hill at your normal climb speed of 150 mph which yields a 595 fpm rate of climb?
Referencing the previous question, by how much will you clear the hill?

choices.text: You will impact 191 feet below the hilltop
You will impact 84 feet below the hilltop
You will clear the hill by 28 feet
You will clear the hill by 188 feet

questions.correctChoice: You will clear the hill by 28 feet
questions.context: Referenced question 23: You must clear a 1,700' hilltop on departure. If you takeoff from sea level and the hill is 6.3 nm away, will you clear the hill at your normal climb speed of 150 mph which yields a 595 fpm rate of climb?

questions.question: What climb rate do you have if you're climbing at 410 ft/sm with a ground speed of 93 knots?

You climb at 97 mph. Your Airport Facility Directory indicates that there is a 91 meter obstacle off the end of the runway and you've calculated it to be 1.1 nm from your point of rotation. What climb rate will be required to clear this obstacle by 50'?
If you cover 90 nm in 70 minutes, what rate of climb do you need to maintain 427 feet per nautical mile climb gradient?
If you are climbing at 550 feet per minute, what is your climb gradient if your ground speed is 84 knots?
While climbing at 860 Ft/NM with a rate of climb of 2330 FPM, what is the ground speed?

Departing QRS airport, there is an obstacle 0.65 NM beyond the point of rotation. This obstacle is 212 feet tall and the pilot wishes to clear it by 50 feet. If the ground speed will be 79 knots, what is the required rate of climb to clear the obstacle as desired?

A pilot is departing Ketchikan, AK (30 feet above sea level) on a true course of 196°, where the altimeter setting is 29.92". There is a hill 6.2 NM away that is 2607 feet above field elevation. If the winds aloft are 230° at 41 kts, and OAT of +30°C, and the indicated airspeed is 120 kts in the climb, what rate of climb is required to clear the obstacle by at least 100 feet?
To maintain a climb rate of 335 feet per nm with a groundspeed of 123 mph, what is the minimum climb rate needed in feet per minute?

You need to clear a hilltop that is 4.5 NM from the end of the runway that is 1700' above the runway elevation. You estimate your groundspee d during the climb to be 87 kts. To clear the hilltop by 500' what" must your minimum rate of climb be?
To maintain a climb rate of 521 ft. per nautical mile at a groundspeed of 107 kts., what minimum vertical rate of climb will you need?

To clear an obstacle 3.4 NM from the runway end you determine that you will need to climb 425' for every nautical mile you travel. At a ground speed of 77 kts., what must your MINIMUM vertical rate of climb be?
How many miles will you cover in 3500 feet of altitude gain?
questions.context: Referenced question 31: At a climb rate of 2400 feet per minute and a ground speed of 105 knots, how many nautical miles will you cover during 5 minutes of climb?

questions.question: To maintain a climb rate of 416 feet per statute mile with a ground speed of 102 mph, what is the minimum rate of climb needed in feet per minute?

Your clearance requires a minimum rate of climb of 267 feet per nautical mile from 1,500 feet to 11,000 feet. You calculate that your aircraft can maintain a climb rate of 720 feet per minute. This will be enough to meet the climb gradient as long as your groundspeed does not exceed...................... _________________________mph (whole number)

You need to clear a hilltop that is 3.1 NM from the end of the runway and is 2300' above the runway elevation. You estimate your groundspeed during the climb to be 78 kts. To clear the hilltop by 500' what must your minimum rate of climb be?
To maintain a climb rate of 620 ft./nautical mile, at a groundspeed of 112 kts., what minimum vertical rate of climb will you need?

To clear a 1350' obstacle 2.9 NM from the runway end at a groundspeed of 81 kts., what must your MINIMUM vertical rate of climb be? _____________________________________ (Whole number) Write your answer on the answer sheet

A takeoff from Runway 10L in Billings, MT requires a climb gradient of 260' / nm in order to clear a set of towers. Assuming a groundspeed of 145 knots, what rate of climb will ensure you will clear the towers?

You are currently level at 7500 ft MSL cruising towards a national park. This national park has a minimum "over-fly" altitude of 9600 ft MSL. At 20 NM from the nearest boundary of the national park, you plan to climb to cross over this boundary at 11,500 ft MSL. If your TAS is 120 knots, on a TC of 123°, and winds aloft of 180° at 22 knots. What rate of climb is needed to proceed as planned?

You are planning a flight to another airport when you discover a new restricted area has been established. It lies directly in your path, 74 nautical miles away. The area occupies the airspace from ground level up to, but not including, FL290. If the departure airport's field elevation is 2000 ft, will you be able to climb to FL290 without violating the restricted area? Assume no wind. Climb capability: [[NEXT_LINE]] Departure airport to 8000': 1500 fpm at 130 KTAS [[NEXT_LINE]] 8000' to FL180: 900 fpm at 120 KTAS [[NEXT_LINE]] FL180 to FL 290: 500 fpm at 110 KTAS

choices.text: Yes, you'll reach FL290 about 3 NM before the restricted area
No, you'll reach FL290 about 3 NM after entering the restricted area
Yes, you'll reach FL290 and cross the lateral limit at the same time
No, you'll reach FL290 about 12 NM after entering the restricted area

questions.correctChoice: Yes, you'll reach FL290 about 3 NM before the restricted area

questions.question: To comply with the runway 15 departure procedure, an aircraft must maintain 350 fpnm during the climb. The procedure says to depart runway heading until reaching 8000 ft. Field elevation is 750 ft. Climb TAS is 90 mph. The winds remain a constant 180@30 kts magnetic. Assuming the pilot stays on the assigned heading, what is the minimum climb rate needed to comply?
Pertaining to problem #26, how far has the aircraft traveled from the airport when it reaches 8000 ft?
questions.context: Referenced question 26: To comply with the runway 15 departure procedure, an aircraft must maintain 350 fpnm during the climb. The procedure says to depart runway heading until reaching 8000 ft. Field elevation is 750 ft. Climb TAS is 90 mph. The winds remain a constant 180@30 kts magnetic. Assuming the pilot stays on the assigned heading, what is the minimum climb rate needed to comply?

questions.question: You are climbing 165 meters every 3 sm. You will be leveling off at 9,500 ft. The GPS says it will take 18 minutes to reach altitude. If the departure airport's elevation is 1,500 ft., what is your groundspeed?
If you maintain the calculated groundspeed from question 3 and a rate of climb of 700 feet per minute, will you be able to climb high enough to clear the TFR?
questions.context: Referenced question 3: You are departing from Camas Municipal Airport, WA heading north to Seattle-Tacoma International Airport, WA. To check your groundspeed you use the few sections of land (1 section of land = 1 statute mile) that exist. If you cross 5 sections of land in 3 minutes and 36 seconds, how fast are you going? [[DIVIDER]] In question 3, you were heading north towards Seattle. At 17.8 nautical miles from the center of Mt. St. Helens at 7400 feet MSL, you realize that you have forgotten about the temporary flight restriction (TFR) up to 13,000 ft MSL within a 5 nautical mile radius around the center of Mt. St. Helens.

questions.question: Departing from an airport (elevation 99 feet), you are to cross over a ridge (elevation 2400 feet) by 500 feet. If your groundspeed is 112 knots and you maintain a 800 feet/minute climb, how far from the ridge are you when you liftoff?
At a climb rate of 400 feet per statute mile or 1300 feet per minute, what is your groundspeed?

Departing Runway 34 at KAVL, field elevation 2165' MSL, you follow the Asheville One Departure procedure that requires a 300' per nm climb gradient to 4800' MSL. If you hold the climb rate of 300' per nm and it takes you 6 minutes 12 seconds to reach 4800' MSL, what is your goundspeed?

choices.text: 154.6 mph Use the following information for questions 33 and 34. Winds: 3000 6000 9000 12000 0000 3612 2718 1830 TAS = 168 mph TRUE COURSE = 042° VAR = 4° WEST Fuel on board = 207 pounds Fuel burn = 14.6 gph
